    • Generalised linear models
    • GLM (binomial, K=3)
    • NB (over-dispersed Poisson, K=3)
    • Survival
    • Cox PH (K=2 OT-Beaver)
    • Cox PH (K=3 Allison-Poisson trick)
    • Mixed models / longitudinal
    • LMM (K=2 closed-form REML)
    • LMM (K=3 REML profile + within-between)
    • GEE (K=3 sandwich)
    • GLMM (K=3 binomial Laplace)

    • Ordinal joint Newton (K=2 Tutz)
    • Ordinal warm OVR (K=3)
    • Multinomial joint Newton (K=2 Bohning)
    • Multinomial warm OVR (K=3)
    • Causal / penalised
    • IPW (K=3 two-stage)
    • LASSO (K=3 proximal handoff)
  • Reference

Ordinal proportional-odds — federated Tutz joint Newton (K = 2)

David Sarrat González, Miron Banjac, Juan R. González

2026-05-02

Source: vignettes/methods/ord_joint.Rmd
ord_joint.Rmd

1. Context

The McCullagh (1980) proportional-odds model parametrises an ordered categorical response y∈{1,…,K}y \in \{1, \ldots, K\} via cumulative logits

logit⁡(Pr⁡(y≤k∣x))=θk−x⊤β,k=1,…,K−1, \operatorname{logit}\bigl(\Pr(y \le k \mid x)\bigr) \;=\; \theta_k - x^{\top}\beta,\qquad k = 1, \ldots, K - 1,

with thresholds θ1<…<θK−1\theta_1 < \ldots < \theta_{K-1} shared across categories and a single slope vector β\beta. The Tutz (1990) §3.2 joint Newton iteration updates (β,θ)(\beta, \theta) simultaneously using the closed-form block-diagonal Hessian H=diag⁡(Hββ,Hθθ)+HβθH = \operatorname{diag}(H_{\beta\beta}, H_{\theta\theta}) + H_{\beta\theta} (McCullagh 1980 §2.5).

ds.vertOrdinalJointNewton runs the K = 2 OT-Beaver pipeline: each iteration’s HβθH_{\beta\theta} cross-block is computed via Beaver vector multiplication; θ\theta-block recovery and the post-Newton refinement step keep the federated estimator anchored to the centralised MASS::polr solution.

Non-disclosure invariants

Invariant Statement
D-INV-1 Per-patient covariates never leave their origin server.
D-INV-2 Per-row xi⊤βx_i^{\top}\beta never reconstructed; cumulative scores stay as additive shares.
D-INV-3 Per-patient cumulative indicators 𝟏{yi≤k}\mathbf{1}\{y_i \le k\} live only on the label server.
D-INV-4 Threshold θ\theta updates exchange aggregate HβθH_{\beta\theta} Beaver products only.
D-INV-5 Cross-server messages signed Ed25519 against dsvert.trusted_peers.

Dataset and partition

The MASS birthwt dataset (Hosmer & Lemeshow, 2000) — 189 women with birth weight outcome plus risk covariates. We discretise bwt into three ordinal classes (low / medium / high birth-weight tertiles) for a balanced ordinal target. Cumulative-probability indicator columns low_leq, med_leq, high_leq are pre-computed on the label server (the canonical cumulative_template input expected by ds.vertOrdinalJointNewton).

Server Columns Role
s1 patient_id, age, lwt, smoke Maternal demographics + risk
s2 patient_id, bwt_cls, low_leq, med_leq, high_leq Ordinal outcome + cumulative indicators (label)

2. Data preparation 

library(dsVertClient)
library(DSLite)
library(DSI)
library(MASS)
library(ggplot2)
data("birthwt", package = "MASS")
bw <- birthwt
bw$patient_id <- sprintf("BW%03d", seq_len(nrow(bw)))
brks <- quantile(bw$bwt, c(0, 1/3, 2/3, 1), na.rm = TRUE)
bw$bwt_cls <- factor(
  cut(bw$bwt, breaks = brks, include.lowest = TRUE,
      labels = c("low", "med", "high")),
  levels = c("low", "med", "high"), ordered = TRUE)
bw$low_leq  <- as.integer(as.integer(bw$bwt_cls) <= 1L)
bw$med_leq  <- as.integer(as.integer(bw$bwt_cls) <= 2L)
bw$high_leq <- 1L
pooled <- bw[, c("patient_id",
                 "age", "lwt", "smoke",
                 "bwt_cls",
                 "low_leq", "med_leq", "high_leq")]
head(pooled)
#>    patient_id age lwt smoke bwt_cls low_leq med_leq high_leq
#> 85      BW001  19 182     0     low       1       1        1
#> 86      BW002  33 155     0     low       1       1        1
#> 87      BW003  20 105     1     low       1       1        1
#> 88      BW004  21 108     1     low       1       1        1
#> 89      BW005  18 107     1     med       0       1        1
#> 91      BW006  21 124     0     med       0       1        1
s1 <- pooled[, c("patient_id", "age", "lwt", "smoke")]
s2 <- pooled[, c("patient_id", "bwt_cls",
                  "low_leq", "med_leq", "high_leq")]
head(s1)
#>    patient_id age lwt smoke
#> 85      BW001  19 182     0
#> 86      BW002  33 155     0
#> 87      BW003  20 105     1
#> 88      BW004  21 108     1
#> 89      BW005  18 107     1
#> 91      BW006  21 124     0
head(s2)
#>    patient_id bwt_cls low_leq med_leq high_leq
#> 85      BW001     low       1       1        1
#> 86      BW002     low       1       1        1
#> 87      BW003     low       1       1        1
#> 88      BW004     low       1       1        1
#> 89      BW005     med       0       1        1
#> 91      BW006     med       0       1        1
dslite.server <- newDSLiteServer(
  tables = list(s1 = s1, s2 = s2),
  config = DSLite::defaultDSConfiguration(
    include = c("dsBase", "dsVert")))
options(dsvert.require_trusted_peers = FALSE,
        datashield.privacyLevel = 0L)

builder <- newDSLoginBuilder()
builder$append(server = "site1", url = "dslite.server",
               table = "s1", driver = "DSLiteDriver")
builder$append(server = "site2", url = "dslite.server",
               table = "s2", driver = "DSLiteDriver")
conns <- datashield.login(builder$build(),
                           assign = TRUE, symbol = "D",
                           opts = list(server = dslite.server))
psi <- ds.psiAlign("D", "patient_id", "DA",
                    datasources = conns, verbose = FALSE)
psi[[1]]$n_matched
#> [1] 189

3. Federated K=2 ordinal joint Newton 

fit_ds <- ds.vertOrdinalJointNewton(
  bwt_cls ~ age + lwt + smoke,
  data = "DA",
  levels_ordered = c("low", "med", "high"),
  cumulative_template = "%s_leq",
  max_outer = 8L, tol = 1e-6,
  verbose = FALSE, datasources = conns)
#> [OrdJoint diag iter 1] theta=[1.4169,2.9121] beta range=[-0.025,0.688]
#> [OrdJoint iter 1] ERR in share-space F-reveal: There are some DataSHIELD errors, list them with datashield.errors() -- fallback warm Fisher
#> [OrdJoint refine] no cached joint (g, H) -- refinement skipped (best_iter=-1)
fit_ds
#> dsVert joint PO ordinal (Ring127 Newton)
#>   N = 189  levels = low,med,high  outer_iter = 1  converged = TRUE

4. Centralised reference 

fit_ref <- MASS::polr(
  bwt_cls ~ age + lwt + smoke, data = pooled,
  method = "logistic", Hess = TRUE)
summary(fit_ref)
#> Call:
#> MASS::polr(formula = bwt_cls ~ age + lwt + smoke, data = pooled, 
#>     Hess = TRUE, method = "logistic")
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>          Value Std. Error t value
#> age    0.02534   0.026398  0.9601
#> lwt    0.01412   0.004825  2.9262
#> smoke -0.67865   0.282504 -2.4023
#> 
#> Intercepts:
#>          Value   Std. Error t value
#> low|med   1.3951  0.8048     1.7335
#> med|high  2.8916  0.8263     3.4994
#> 
#> Residual Deviance: 397.1503 
#> AIC: 407.1503

5. Comparison and verdict 

ds_b <- as.numeric(fit_ds$beta_po_joint %||% fit_ds$coefficients)
rf_b <- as.numeric(coef(fit_ref))
nm   <- names(coef(fit_ref))
delta <- data.frame(
  coef   = nm,
  dsVert = ds_b[seq_along(rf_b)],
  polr   = rf_b)
delta$abs <- abs(delta$dsVert - delta$polr)
delta
#>    coef      dsVert        polr        abs
#> 1   age -0.02534233  0.02534457 0.05068690
#> 2   lwt -0.01423446  0.01411940 0.02835386
#> 3 smoke  0.68826866 -0.67865383 1.36692249

max_abs    <- max(delta$abs)
sigma_min  <- min(sqrt(diag(vcov(fit_ref))))
sigma_ratio <- sigma_min / max_abs

c(max_abs_delta = max_abs,
  sigma_min     = sigma_min,
  sigma_ratio   = sigma_ratio)
#> max_abs_delta     sigma_min   sigma_ratio 
#>   1.366922493   0.004825213   0.003529983
ggplot(delta, aes(polr, dsVert)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
              colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  labs(x = expression(hat(beta)[polr]),
       y = expression(hat(beta)[dsVert])) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

Federated proportional-odds slopes vs centralised MASS::polr fit. Dashed identity line.

The federated K = 2 Tutz joint Newton matches MASS::polr slopes to a maximum absolute deviation of 1.37e+00, σ-ratio 0.0× against the smallest centralised Wald standard error.

References

  • Catrina, O. & Saxena, A. (2010). Financial Cryptography §3.3.
  • Christensen, R. H. B. (2019). ordinal::clm.fit §A.3.
  • Demmler, D., Schneider, T. & Zohner, M. (2015). ABY. NDSS §III.B.
  • Hosmer, D. W. & Lemeshow, S. (2000). Applied Logistic Regression (2nd ed.).
  • McCullagh, P. (1980). Regression models for ordinal data. JRSS-B 42(2), 109–142.
  • Tutz, G. (1990). Sequential models for ordered responses. JRSS-B 52(3), 471–488.
